hook: Signless 1944 Barceloneta bodega where the 'bomba' tapa was born.
facts: Working-class tapas bar in La Barceloneta open since 1944, run by the Quimet family across four generations.
It has no sign over the door, an old wood-and-marble interior barely changed in over 75 years.
Widely credited as the birthplace of the 'bomba' — a deep-fried potato-and-meat croquette served with spicy and garlic-aioli sauces.
Address is Carrer del Baluard 56, 08003 Barcelona, a few minutes' walk from the beach.
Known for vermut and market-fresh seafood cooked simply over a charcoal stove.

sources.note: History since 1944, no sign, birthplace of the bomba
Official site; address and history
Listing corroborating Carrer del Baluard, Barceloneta location (coords)

w: No sign over the door in Barceloneta, just a wood-and-marble room barely touched since 1944 and four generations of the Quimet family at the charcoal stove. The bomba was born here — a fried potato-and-meat ball under spicy and garlic sauce, the city's most copied tapa. Vermut, market seafood, no fuss. Find the unmarked door.

hook: 1870 Raval milk-bar where Cacaolat was invented; five generations of chocolate.
facts: Granja (traditional dairy café) founded in 1870 on Carrer d'en Xuclà 4 in El Raval, listed as Architectural Heritage of Catalonia.
Birthplace of Cacaolat, the chocolate-milk drink created by Joan Viader in the early 1930s after a 1931 trip to Hungary.
Still a family business across five generations, known for thick hot chocolate, churros and house-made dairy.
The Viaders historically pasteurised their own milk sourced from Cardedeu, their home town.
Address is Carrer d'en Xuclà 4, 08001 Barcelona.

sources.note: Official site; 1870 founding, address, family history
Time Out: creators of Cacaolat, historic café
El Raval location corroboration (coords)

w: A traditional granja on a Raval side street since 1870, where five generations have poured thick hot chocolate and churros. Cacaolat — Spain's beloved bottled chocolate milk — was invented here in the 1930s by Joan Viader. Marble, mirrors, heritage-listed and barely changed. The original chocolate-milk bar, still in the family.

hook: Barcelona's oldest bar (1820), a dusty absinthe temple of Picasso and Hemingway lore.
facts: Founded in 1820, widely cited as the oldest bar in Barcelona, in El Raval on Carrer de Sant Pau 65.
Specialises in absinthe served the traditional way — sugar cube, slotted spoon, fork and water.
Local lore links the bar to Picasso, Dalí, Gaudí and Hemingway as past patrons.
Interior is famously unchanged: cracked walls, antique chandelier, marble tables and dusty bottle-lined shelves.
Address is Carrer de Sant Pau 65, 08001 Barcelona.
legend: Hemingway is said to have written parts of 'The Sun Also Rises' at one of its marble tables — an oft-repeated but unverified claim.

sources.note: Time Out: 1820 absinthe bar, oldest in the city
19th-century absinthe bar, historic clientele, Sant Pau address (coords)

w: Barcelona's oldest bar, open since 1820 and seemingly undusted since — cracked walls, a tarnished chandelier, marble tables, green absinthe bottles by the rank. They set you up the old way: sugar cube, slotted spoon, a trickle of water. Picasso drank here; Hemingway, the story goes, wrote here too. Probably not. Order the absinthe anyway.

hook: A tiny 1914 family bodega in Poble-sec stacked to the ceiling with wine and conservas.
facts: Family bodega founded in 1914 as a wine shop, now run by the same family across five generations in Poble-sec.
Standing-room only: a tiny space with no chairs, holding only about 30 people, designed for eating at the counter.
Famous for 'montaditos' — layered open sandwiches built on tinned seafood and high-quality conservas.
Address is Carrer del Poeta Cabanyes 25, 08004 Barcelona.
The original wine came from a village near Montserrat where the founder's great-grandfather made it.

sources.note: Official site; founded 1914, family history
Time Out: institution of vermut/bodega culture in Poble-sec (coords)

w: A standing-room bodega in Poble-sec, in the same family since 1914 and stacked floor to ceiling with bottles — room for about thirty, and not a chair in sight. The thing to order: montaditos, little open sandwiches built on top-grade tinned seafood. Wine off the shelf, food on your feet. Elbow in and point.

hook: The hidden-passage roastery that lit Barcelona's third-wave coffee scene.
facts: Founded in 2014 by Barcelona-born barista and roaster Jordi Mestre, a former Spanish National Barista Champion.
Widely credited as a pioneer of Barcelona's third-wave / specialty coffee scene.
Its landmark Lab & Shop sits on Passatge de Sert, a small passageway between El Born and Plaça de Catalunya.
Mestre trained at Nude Espresso in London before returning to Barcelona to roast single-origin coffees.
Address is Passatge de Sert 12, 08003 Barcelona.

sources.note: Official site; locations and address
Sprudge: founded 2014 by Jordi Mestre, pioneer of specialty coffee, Passatge Sert (coords)

w: Tucked down the Passatge de Sert, the roastery that helped wake Barcelona up to specialty coffee — opened in 2014 by Jordi Mestre, a former national barista champion who trained in London before coming home to roast. Single origins, pulled with care, down a passage you could walk straight past. Where the city's third wave began.

hook: Barcelona's oldest garden — an 18th-century neoclassical park with a real cypress maze.
facts: The oldest conserved historical garden in Barcelona; construction began in 1791 and the first phase finished in 1808.
Built on the former Desvalls family estate at the foot of Collserola, designed by Italian architect Domenico Bagutti.
Contains the only hedge maze in the city — a cypress labyrinth and one of the few so old in Spain.
Combines an 18th-century neoclassical garden with a 19th-century romantic garden.
Catalogued as a cultural asset of local interest (BCIL) and managed as a museum garden with controlled access.

sources.note: Construction 1791-1808, Desvalls estate, Bagutti, coordinates
betevé: the oldest garden in Barcelona

w: Barcelona's oldest garden, begun in 1791 on the Desvalls estate below Collserola — a neoclassical terrace above, a romantic 19th-century garden below, and at its centre the only hedge maze in the city. An Italian architect cut the cypress labyrinth, built to get you pleasantly lost. A quiet escape, far up in Horta.

hook: Spain's oldest jazz cellar under Plaça Reial, where Chet Baker and Ornette Coleman played.
facts: Opened in 1960 in a cellar under Plaça Reial by Joan Rosselló, who converted the former Bar Brindis; it is the oldest jazz venue in Spain.
Its 1960s golden era hosted Chet Baker, Ornette Coleman, Dexter Gordon, Stéphane Grappelli, Kenny Clarke and more.
The club closed in 1968 and was reopened in 1993 by music promoters Mas i Mas.
'Jamboree' is a Zulu-derived word glossed by the club as 'a gathering of tribes'.
Located at Plaça Reial 17, 08002 Barcelona, in the Barri Gòtic.

sources.note: Europe Jazz Network: history, 1960 opening, artists
Plaça Reial location and history (coords)

w: Spain's oldest jazz club, opened in 1960 in a stone cellar under Plaça Reial — and in its first decade Chet Baker, Ornette Coleman, Dexter Gordon and Stéphane Grappelli all played down here. It went dark in 1968 and came back in 1993. The name, the club says, is Zulu for a gathering of tribes. Still where Barcelona swings.

hook: A collector's paradise on record-shop row, with its own reissue label of lost gems.
facts: Independent record shop founded in 1992 by Jordi Segura on Carrer de la Riera Baixa 14, El Raval's record-shop street.
Stocks originals and reissues across blues, jazz, soul, funk, garage, psych, prog and more, arranged in dense 'organised chaos'.
Segura also runs the Wah Wah Records reissue label, with more than 300 references of rare and obscure music.
Profiled by The Vinyl Factory in its 'world's best record shops' series.
Address is Carrer de la Riera Baixa 14, 08001 Barcelona.

sources.note: The Vinyl Factory: history, founder, label
Riera Baixa 14, El Raval location (coords)

w: A collector's record shop on Riera Baixa, the Raval's street of vinyl, dealing originals and reissues across blues, jazz, soul, funk, garage and psych since 1992 — organised chaos, deep crates. Owner Jordi Segura runs his own reissue label, 300-plus rescues of obscure records. The Vinyl Factory named it one of the world's best.

hook: A 400-metre Civil War air-raid shelter dug into Montjuïc by Poble-sec's own residents.
facts: A Spanish Civil War air-raid shelter dug by local residents from 1937 to 1939 into the slope of Montjuïc.
Named simply as the 307th shelter registered with Barcelona city hall; one of the best preserved of roughly 1,300 in the city.
Comprises nearly 400 metres of tunnels about 2.1 m high, with an infirmary, toilets, a children's room and a water fountain; it could hold around 2,000 people.
Walls still bear wartime inscriptions such as bans on spreading pessimism or talking about politics.
Now part of the MUHBA (Barcelona History Museum); the three entrances are on Carrer Nou de la Rambla 169.

sources.note: MUHBA official: history, tunnels, capacity
Nou de la Rambla, Poble-sec / Montjuïc location (coords)

w: Four hundred metres of tunnel dug into Montjuïc by the people of Poble-sec themselves, between 1937 and 1939, to shelter from Franco's bombs — the 307th air-raid refuge on the city's register, one of the best kept of some 1,300. An infirmary, a children's room, a fountain, and walls still warning against pessimism or politics.
